claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To start determining your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions about your child.

**First, how old is your child?**

This is important because:
- Children **under 16** generally qualify automatically
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** don't qualify for Child Benefit

Please tell me your child's age so we can continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, yes I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit. I have one child - Alex, who is 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information! Let me continue with the assessment for Alex, who is 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news! Since Alex is 8 years old (under 16), they fall into the category that generally qualifies for Child Benefit.

Now, the next question:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

For an 8-year-old, this would typically be "yes" - children of this age are rarely in paid work. Can you confirm that Alex is either not working or working less than 24 hours per week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, Alex doesn't work at all - he's just a kid!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That makes sense for an 8-year-old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

In other words, is Alex receiving benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit in his own name?

For an 8-year-old, this would typically be "no" - meaning Alex is NOT receiving these benefits in his own right. Can you confirm that Alex is not claiming ESA or Universal Credit in his own name?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in his own name - he's just 8, so everything goes through me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's what I would expect. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Next question:

**Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship?**

At 8 years old, Alex wouldn't be in an apprenticeship program, but I need to confirm. Apprenticeships can affect Child Benefit eligibility depending on the type and region (England, Scotland, or Wales).

Can you confirm that Alex is NOT in any apprenticeship program?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, of course not - he's only 8! He's in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely right! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now we need to check your residency status. This is an important requirement for Child Benefit.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You'll meet this requirement if **ANY** of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively looking for work (up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support your family
6. You have pre-settled status as a student with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do any of these apply to you?** Most UK residents will meet the first criterion - normally living in the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - we're in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Living in Manchester means you meet the UK residency requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to confirm your responsibility for Alex.

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You're considered responsible if **ANY** of these apply:

1. **You live with Alex**
2. You contribute to Alex's upkeep (paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit or equivalent in kind - including food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

Since you mentioned "everything goes through me" earlier, I'm assuming you live with Alex. Can you confirm that you live with him?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now an important question about other potential claimants:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

This is important because only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time. For example, if Alex has another parent or guardian, are they currently claiming Child Benefit for him?

Please let me know:
- **No one else is claiming** for Alex, OR
- Someone else is claiming but you've **agreed to transfer** the claim to you, OR
- There's a **dispute** about who should claim

Which situation applies to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check Alex's living situation to see if any special rules apply.

**Which of these best describes Alex's current situation?**

1. **Alex is in hospital** (as an inpatient)
2. **Alex is in care** (looked after by the local authority)
3. **Alex is fostered**
4. **Alex is living normally at home** with you

Based on what you've told me (that Alex lives at home with you), I believe it's option 4 - living normally at home. Can you confirm this is correct, and that Alex is not currently in hospital, in care, or fostered?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, Alex is just living normally at home with me - he's not in hospital, care, or fostered.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Just one final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is the local council paying for Alex's living costs or upkeep? 

Since Alex lives at home with you and you're responsible for him, I would expect the answer to be "yes" - meaning the council is NOT paying for Alex. Can you confirm that the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct - the council isn't paying anything towards Alex. I cover everything for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
# 🎉 Great News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

**Result: ELIGIBLE**

You meet all the Child Benefit eligibility requirements for Alex.

## Why You're Eligible:

✅ **Alex qualifies as a child** - He's 8 years old (under 16)  
✅ **Not in full-time work** - Alex doesn't work  
✅ **Not receiving benefits** - Alex isn't claiming ESA or Universal Credit in his own name  
✅ **Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship** - Alex is in primary school  
✅ **You meet residency requirements** - You live in Manchester, UK  
✅ **You're responsible for Alex** - He lives at home with you  
✅ **No competing claims** - Nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex  
✅ **Not council-funded** - The local council isn't paying for Alex's upkeep  

## Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate

## Important Information:

⚠️ **High Income Child Benefit Charge**: If you or your partner earn over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay back some or all of the Child Benefit through the tax system. However, you can still claim - it helps protect your National Insurance record.

💷 **Payment**: Child Benefit is normally paid every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits).

## How to Apply:

Visit: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

If you have any questions about the application process, feel free to ask!

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), not in paid work, not receiving benefits in his own right, not in an apprenticeship, and living normally at home. The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), lives with Alex and is responsible for him, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 8-year-old child Alex, who qualifies as a qualifying child under age 16, and the claimant satisfies all residency and responsibility requirements.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), not in paid work, not receiving benefits in his own right, not in an apprenticeship, and living normally at home. The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), lives with Alex and is responsible for him, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 8-year-old child Alex, who qualifies as a qualifying child under age 16, and the claimant satisfies all residency and responsibility requirements.
